does: This paper introduces M-HalDetect, a comprehensive dataset for detecting and preventing hallucinations in large vision language models. The dataset contains 16,000 fine-grained annotations on Visual Question Answering examples, categorizing hallucinations into three types: non-existent objects, unfaithful entity descriptions, and inaccurate relationships. The authors conduct an empirical study showing that state-of-the-art instruction-tuned LVLMs like InstructBLIP produce approximately 30 percent hallucinatory content in detailed visual descriptions. By providing granular annotations that extend beyond object hallucinations to include descriptive and relational errors, the dataset enables systematic evaluation of hallucination detection methods and benchmarking of models designed to mitigate this problem in vision-language tasks.
connects: This work addresses a critical limitation in the rapidly developing field of vision language models that has grown from foundational work like CLIP and BLIP toward instruction-tuned variants. It builds on earlier observations about hallucination in large language models and extends those insights to the multimodal setting, where visual grounding is essential. The paper responds to the gap in existing hallucination research, which has primarily focused on object-level errors in image captioning and VQA tasks. By creating the first large-scale, fine-grained hallucination annotation dataset for vision language models, it complements concurrent efforts to understand model reliability and connects to broader work on model interpretability, factuality checking, and the robustness of neural vision-language systems.
enables: This dataset foundation enables multiple future research directions including the development of specialized hallucination detection classifiers trained on fine-grained error categories, methods for real-time hallucination prevention during generation, and improved instruction-tuning approaches that explicitly mitigate unfaithful outputs. The categorization of hallucination types suggests future work on targeted interventions for different error sources. Additionally, this resource facilitates research into mechanistic understanding of why vision language models produce hallucinations and could support development of more reliable multimodal systems for high-stakes applications like medical imaging analysis or autonomous systems where visual accuracy is critical. It also opens paths toward human-in-the-loop refinement strategies and robustness evaluation frameworks for vision language models.
